author: @icebluscorpion
text: Could you pump the water around without taking it out and dipping it right in to eliminate the variable charges in environment. What if this dipping and redipping ist some sort of electronic reluctance. Where you get only a change in reading by variing the conductivity in between the plates?

author: @dinskiy
text:
Fantastic work as always.. 
I just wanted to point out that sea water is not only NaCl, it is a mix of slats including MgCL..   Mg2+ and Cl 2- 
This can probably replace the copper chloride that you are looking at and give you the same results..  
DEAD SEA Salt is very high in Magnesium chloride.. 
I'll have been trying this on a test unit over here and it is very promising..

Oh ok you did that in the next video..

author: @dpconnell
text:
Looks promising. Keep in mind that (as you know) hot water holds more salt, which could tap into the thermosiphon idea someone else mentioned.
If it's a closed system acting as a generator, how would you recharge the CuCl? Unless that's a naive question based on my not knowing much about chemistry...

replies.author: @ThinkingandTinkering
replies.text: The chloride doesn't need recharging mate - it's not a battery

replies.author: @dpconnell
replies.text: Isn't it kind of a capacitor tho, if it's electrodes and an ionic fluid?

replies.author: @ThinkingandTinkering
replies.text:
Daniel Connell
sure - there's bound to be an element of that but ion movement will take care of the 'charging' - you might want to review the paper - the link is in one of the vids - the first or second i forget which

author: @jfausset
text:
Excellent work as always.  I, too,  am addicted to your videos.  The sealed system is an awesome idea.  I think I even saw FetchitnowUK or someone else illustrate an idea to use this idea or the thermocell in a thermosyphon scenario, where the pump is fluid temperature differentials.  I think that's pretty cool.  In thinking through the thermocell, which fascinates me a little more because of its operational simplicity, the question of the first law of thermodynamics keeps bugging me: if the input is ambient temperature, shouldn't we see a temperature drop through the thermocell, similar to the Peltier/Seebeck Effect?  The mechanical input with the SWAG makes it less difficult to explain.  

Thanks for these videos.  They are really great hope/inspiration to me.

replies.author: @ThinkingandTinkering
replies.text: cheers mate - I strongly suspect the thermocell works like a solar cell but on IR as for the sealed idea it wasn't mine - it was suggested to me by several people

replies.author: @ThinkingandTinkering
replies.text: cheers mate

author: @joeglory425
text: I'm addicted to watching your videos.  Copper Chloride is to be a salt that intercalates with graphite.  What if the intercalation process generates a voltage and once all the intercalation sites are used up the voltage goes away?  Try soaking your SWAG plates in the copper chloride solution for a day or two and then repeat the experiment to see if there's any change in voltage readings.

replies.author: @ThinkingandTinkering
replies.text: will do

author: @TheBaconWizard
text:
Presumably at some stage it'll also come down to what speed the brine is flowing over the plates as well ?
Not sure I can think of an application just now so probably a dead-end, but sound waves?

replies.author: @ThinkingandTinkering
replies.text: I was thinking of it as a power generator - there are a lot of ways to rig that up. sea acting as a pump, air driven pump (venturi?) mechanical pump there are a ton of ways I don't think it needs to be too complicated

replies.author: @TheBaconWizard
replies.text: Robert Murray-Smith Oh I didn't mean the SWAG is a dead-end, patently NOT. I meant using sound-waves is interesting and perhaps you hadn't thought of it, but it's probably too complicated (and it's trying to find an application for a cool idea, not the other way round which I usually try to avoid)
